draft.introduction: Mumonkan Case 2: “Hyakujo’s Fox.” A monk who taught that an enlightened person is not subject to cause and effect was reborn five hundred times as a fox. Hyakujo corrects the error with a single word.

translation.text: Whenever Hyakujo delivered a sermon, an old man was always there listening. One day the old man said: “In the past, a student asked me whether an enlightened person falls under the law of cause and effect. I answered: No. For this I was reborn as a fox for five hundred lifetimes. Please say a turning word for me.” Hyakujo said: “An enlightened person is not blind to cause and effect.” At these words, the old man was enlightened.
translation.original_text: 百丈凡参、有一老人常随衆听法

translator_notes.note: The distinction between “not falling under” and “not being blind to” cause and effect is a single character’s difference in Chinese. Enlightenment does not exempt one from causation; it enables clear seeing within causation. This is Zen’s corrective to antinomian readings of emptiness.
